subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

reviews.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the macro name might be a concern and is considering alternative names such as __knonnull() or __nonnull_args(), but prefers the latter due to its descriptive nature.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes, a different name might work as well. The main question is which
name to pick.

Plain nonnull() is a bit dangerous. It might collide with existing
identifiers (now or in the future) and is not great for a kernel-wide
macro. I think we got away with plain noinline, because it was there
forever. There are also at least 24 nonnull attribute usages in the
kernel that need to be converted atomically. This can be done, but it
increases the scope of the patchset, which I'd rather avoid.

___nonnull() with triple underscore might be a bit confusing, I agree.

__knonnull() might work. I like __nonnull_args() better: it gives the
reader a hint about the semantics, while __knonnull() is just a
collision-avoidance trick without conveying any additional meaning. That
being said, I can totally do __knonnull().

2026-05-27.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull(), which tells the compiler to warn when a NULL pointer is statically known to be passed. This is done by re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull() for DEFINE_GUARD() and directly annotating it for __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(). The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following existing conventions. This change aims to improve code quality by catching potential NULL pointer issues at compile-time.
